title: Hunting for a New Hallucinogen in the Lilliputian Psychedelic
gist: University of Utah researchers found that Lanmaoa mushrooms, which trigger vivid 'Lilliputian' hallucinations of tiny people, lack the genes for psilocybin and ibotenic acid, implying they contain an entirely novel psychedelic compound unknown to science.

insights.claim: Lilliputian hallucinations, visions of hordes of tiny affable people moving through one's real environment, were first named in 1909 by French psychiatrist Raoul Leroy.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Historical framing of a specific, replicable perceptual phenomenon.

insights.claim: Lilliputian hallucinations are associated with certain neurological conditions and with alcohol withdrawal, and can be induced by eating mushrooms of the genus Lanmaoa, found worldwide.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Ties a specific fungal genus to a defined psychiatric effect.

insights.claim: University of Utah scientists built the first family tree of the Lanmaoa genus and found these mushrooms lack the genes producing psilocybin or ibotenic acid, the two known fungal psychedelics.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: Rules out known compounds, isolating the search space.

insights.claim: Because Lanmaoa produces psychedelic effects without psilocybin or ibotenic acid, researchers conclude it must contain a totally novel hallucinogenic compound unlike any known to science.
insights.speaker: Kristen French
insights.so_what: A genuinely new psychoactive scaffold is a potential drug-discovery lead worth tracking.
insights.quote: Lanmaoa must contain a totally novel psychedelic compound
